# Release Notes

## July 4, 2026

- **Improvement — The automated Devflow Review now waits for a fresh branch and green CI before spending an LLM review** — Two new settings, `devflow_review.require_up_to_date` and `devflow_review.require_ci_green` (both enabled by default), defer the automatic review while the pull request branch is behind its base or while any other CI signal on the head commit is still pending or failing. A deferred review posts the required `Devflow Review` check completed as neutral with a "waiting" reason — so the required check is never missing and merging is not blocked by the deferral itself — and the review re-triggers automatically when the branch is updated or CI completes. A repository with no other CI is reviewed immediately, the check's Re-run button forces a review at any time, and setting either key to `false` restores the unconditional trigger. Consumer repositories must name their CI workflows in `devflow-review.yml`'s `workflow_run` trigger list (a GitHub platform requirement) for the Actions-CI re-trigger to fire. (#307)
- **Improvement — `/devflow:create-issue` now stress-tests its own draft before you see it** — A new mandatory self-steelman step re-reads the assembled draft as an adversarial reviewer and verifies every load-bearing claim, file reference, and acceptance criterion against the actual code with targeted reads and searches, hunting for missed acceptance criteria, missed edge cases, wrong assumptions, and unstated scope. The skill revises the draft itself, re-runs its no-options gate, and reports a one-line outcome summary, so flaws are fixed before the draft is presented rather than left for you to catch. (#307)

## July 3, 2026

- **Fix — Skill safety checks now fire correctly on non-Claude-Code runners** — When running DevFlow on agentic command-line tools other than Claude Code (such as GitHub Copilot CLI, Cursor, Codex CLI, or Gemini CLI), the internal guard checks inside the skills read an empty result because those tools drop a value passed between two steps of the same inline command. As a result, warning messages and fail-safe branches silently never ran — most notably the documentation gate in `/devflow:implement`, which stopped enforcing that required documentation was actually updated. Every such check has been rewritten into a single-statement form that reads the command's own result inline, so the safety checks and their warnings now hold on these runners, and behavior on Claude Code is unchanged. (#286)
- **Fix — Cloud stall backstop is now resilient to expired GitHub App tokens** — On a long cloud `/devflow:implement` run, the App installation token could expire before the stall backstop read the workpad, causing the backstop to misread a perfectly healthy run as corrupt and waste one of its limited automatic resume attempts. The backstop now mints a fresh token just before it runs, and treats any authentication or API failure while reading the workpad as a distinct condition that fails the run loud with a clear diagnostic **without** consuming a resume attempt — so a transient token or API problem is never mistaken for a broken workpad. (#287)
- **Improvement — DevFlow reviews now post under a dedicated `DevFlow-Reviewer` identity so the review never collides with the PR author** — GitHub does not allow requesting changes on or approving your own pull request, so when the review agent ran under the same GitHub App that DevFlow uses to author PRs, its formal verdict could not post as a visible review. Every review path — the automated cloud reviewer and the manual `/devflow:review` command — now runs under a separate `DevFlow-Reviewer` GitHub App when you configure the `DEVFLOW_REVIEWER_APP_ID` variable and `DEVFLOW_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY` secret (a downscoped, read-only-on-code identity), otherwise `github-actions[bot]`, and never the PR-authoring App. If you already run DevFlow with a single App, review attribution moves to `github-actions[bot]` until you set up `DevFlow-Reviewer`; see `docs/cloud-setup.md`. (#303)
- **Fix — The `/devflow:implement` run link now stays current across stall and retry resumes** — When a cloud run was re-triggered or automatically resumed after a stall, the "Run" link on the issue workpad kept pointing at the original run, so an operator could not click through to the logs of the job that was actually active. The early-acknowledgement step now rewrites that link to the current run on every resume, and the draft pull request it opens also links back to the run that created it (omitted on a local run, where there is no such link), making a mid-run pull request easy to trace back to its originating job. (#302)
- **Fix — DevFlow skills now load config and prompt extensions when run from a repository subdirectory** — Previously, when a `/devflow:*` skill was invoked from a folder below the repository root (common in large or monorepo layouts), DevFlow resolved the `.devflow/` directory relative to the current folder and silently fell back to built-in defaults, losing the team's committed configuration and prompt extensions with no error or warning. DevFlow now anchors the default `.devflow/` path to the git repository root, so committed config and prompt extensions load correctly no matter which subdirectory a skill is run from. A nested submodule or inner repository, or a monorepo whose `.devflow/` is deliberately not at the git root, is not covered. (#299)

## July 2, 2026

- **Fix — Cloud stall backstop now fails closed on an unreadable workpad status** — The stall backstop that watches cloud `/devflow:implement` runs could previously misread a corrupted or unrecognized workpad status as a healthy in-progress run, silently spending one of its limited automatic resume attempts instead of flagging the problem. It now recognizes that condition as unreadable and fails the run loud with a diagnostic comment, the same way it already handled a missing status, so automatic resume attempts are no longer wasted on runs it cannot actually read. (#283)
